description_long: This is a modern, self-balancing hoverboard with a sleek, glossy black exterior and a streamlined, symmetrical design. It features two large, circular wheels on either side, each encased in a robust housing, and a central platform with two textured footpads for user stability. The footpads are slightly raised and appear to have a grippy surface, likely made of rubber or a similar material, to prevent slipping. Small LED indicator lights are visible near the center, suggesting battery or status feedback. The overall construction appears sturdy, with a combination of hard plastic and metal components, and the branding 'RAWBOARDS' is visible on one side. The hoverboard is compact and designed for personal transportation in urban environments.
description: A glossy black hoverboard with large side wheels, textured footpads, LED indicators, and visible branding. Its streamlined, sturdy build is ideal for urban personal transport.
description_view_0: Side view showing the full length, both footpads, and the central LED indicator.
description_view_1: End-on view highlighting one large wheel and the circular hubcap design.
description_view_2: Opposite side view with visible 'RAWBOARDS' branding and both footpads.
description_view_3: End-on view from the opposite wheel, mirroring view 1 with the same wheel design.
attribute_wheel_configuration: Two large, parallel wheels located at each end of the board, fully exposed with circular hubcaps; wheels are of equal size and dominate the side profile.
attribute_footpad_design: Two rectangular footpads, one on each side of the central axis, slightly raised with a textured, grippy rubber surface; designed for secure footing and likely embedded with pressure sensors.
attribute_lighting_features: Small green LED indicator lights near the center, likely for status or battery indication.
attribute_brand_logo: A 'RAWBOARDS' logo in white and red is visible on one side of the board, near the footpad.
attribute_control_interface: No visible external control interface; operation is likely through footpad sensors and internal gyroscopes.
